contentHtml:
• Sub-Header: Combining Manual Audits, Automated Scans, and Continuous Monitoring.
• Speaker Info: Will Kirchheimer
• Visual Note: Simple, professional title card.

• Body Text:
• More than 90% of websites today would fail a basic WCAG 2.1 AA audit.
• WCAG 2.1 AA is no longer just a "best practice"—it is the baseline legal standard for navigating the digital world.
• Why? Because the digital landscape is where modern life happens: healthcare, employment, education, and civic duty.

slideName: Slide 3: Who Are We Really Serving? (Beyond Screen Readers)
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• Visual: Blindness, low vision, color blindness.
• Auditory: Deafness and hearing loss.
• Motor & Physical: Inability to use a mouse, tremors, paralysis.
• Cognitive & Neurological: ADHD, Dyslexia, Autism, long-COVID brain fog.
• Temporary/Situational: A broken arm, bright sunlight, loud rooms without headphones.

• Body Text:
• Accessibility isn't just about color contrast and alt tags.
• It's about minimizing cognitive load: reducing animation motion, providing pausing mechanisms, and using clear, brutalist UX writing.
• Neurodivergent users rely on predictable layouts and explicit error messages.

slideName: Slide 5: The Hidden ROI of WCAG
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• SEO Penetration: Semantic HTML and perfect heading hierarchies map directly to Google's Core Web Vitals.
• AI Training & Machine Readability: WCAG standards structure data perfectly for LLMs and AI web-scrapers to ingest and cite your content.
• Expanded Market Reach: Tapping into a $1.2 trillion disabled consumer market.

slideName: Slide 6: A "Hacker" Perspective on Bad Code
header: The Carbon Footprint of Inaccessible DOMs
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• Bloated, non-semantic HTML and massive javascript overlay widgets (like accessiBe) aren't just accessibility failures.
• They drastically increase the processing power required to render a page.
• Clean, semantic, WCAG-compliant HTML uses less energy on servers and client devices. Accessibility is inherently green.

section: PART 2: The Rising Legal Tsunami (15-35 Minutes)
slideName: Slide 7: The Legal Reality - Title II vs. Title III
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• Title II (State & Local Government): The Hard Deadline. The DOJ mandates WCAG 2.1 AA compliance by April 24, 2026.
• Title III (Private Sector): The Litigation Trap. The surge of private lawsuits driven by the "Nexus Standard."

section: PART 2: The Rising Legal Tsunami (15-35 Minutes)
slideName: Slide 8: Title II Targets (Audience Interaction Option 1)
header: Who is in the Title II Crosshairs?
contentHtml:
• Body Text (List):
• Chicago Public Schools (CPS)
• University of Illinois System
• Illinois Secretary of State (DMV)
• City of Chicago Municipal Portals
• Public Libraries & County Courthouses
• Speaker Prompt: Ask the crowd: *"Why are these specific groups at such massive legal risk right now?"*

section: PART 2: The Rising Legal Tsunami (15-35 Minutes)
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• The April 2026 mandate doesn't just apply to public-facing marketing sites.
• It strictly regulates internal portals, online learning platforms (Canvas/Blackboard), digital syllabi/PDFs, and even third-party vendor applications.
• If a university buys third-party software, the university holds the liability.

section: PART 2: The Rising Legal Tsunami (15-35 Minutes)
header: What Does a DOJ Lawsuit Look Like?
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• State of Alaska: Sued for failing to provide an accessible digital ballot.
• City of Los Angeles: Class-action for inaccessible parking ticket payment portals and park reservations systems.
• Service Oklahoma: Sued because the official DMV mobile app was functionally inaccessible to screen readers (missing ARIA labels).

section: PART 2: The Rising Legal Tsunami (15-35 Minutes)
header: The Title II "Interim Final Rule" (IFR) Curveball
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• While the April 2026 deadline is firm, the DOJ quietly sent an Interim Final Rule to OIRA in early 2026.
• This allows the DOJ to alter or soften provisions *without* public comment right before the deadline hits, keeping compliance teams guessing.

section: PART 2: The Rising Legal Tsunami (15-35 Minutes)
slideName: Slide 12: Title III Targets (Audience Interaction Option 2)
header: Who is in the Title III Crosshairs?
contentHtml:
• Body Text (List):
• Ticketmaster / Live Nation
• Marriott International
• Hilton / Hilton Honors
• Expedia / Booking.com
• Home Depot / E-Commerce
• Speaker Prompt: Ask the crowd: *"Why are these massive private companies so legally vulnerable when they don't have a 2026 deadline?"*

section: PART 2: The Rising Legal Tsunami (15-35 Minutes)
header: The Nexus Standard & The AI Pro Se Surge
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• The Nexus Standard: Courts rule that if a website is a gateway to a physical location (a Hilton hotel, an arena), it is a Place of Public Accommodation.
• The Pro Se Surge: A 40% spike in unrepresented individuals using AI tools to draft airtight federal ADA complaints.
• Complex Booking Engines: These sites fail because interactive seating charts, dynamic inventory, and pop-up date-pickers trap screen readers.

section: PART 2: The Rising Legal Tsunami (15-35 Minutes)
slideName: Slide 14: Sector Deep-Dive: The HHS Mandate
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• The DOJ isn’t the only enforcer. The Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) issued a massive, separate deadline.
• Healthcare websites, patient charting portals, and medical mobile apps must meet WCAG 2.1 Level AA standards by May 2026.

section: PART 2: The Rising Legal Tsunami (15-35 Minutes)
slideName: Slide 15: The Futility of Title III Legal Defenses
header: What Happens When You Get Sued?
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• Defense 1: "Mootness" (We hired a dev firm to fix it!) - Fails. Requires proving the site is *already* 100% fixed.
• Defense 2: "Primary Jurisdiction" (We are waiting on DOJ rules!) - Routinely rejected by courts.
• Defense 3: "Ignorance" & Overlays - Installing an AI widget (like accessiBe) does not hold up in court if the underlying code remains broken.

section: PART 2: The Rising Legal Tsunami (15-35 Minutes)
slideName: Slide 16: The Harsh Reality of Litigation
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• Because common legal defenses fail in court, businesses have almost no viable defense when non-compliant code is structurally verified.
• Litigating a verified WCAG failure costs exponentially more in plaintiff attorney fees than simply paying the settlement and fixing the code.

slideName: Slide 17: Vulnerability: The Complex Intake Form
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• Complex web forms are the most common source of Title III liability.
• Missing tags, improper error state announcements, and keyboard trapping.
• Primary Targets: FAFSA applications, multi-step mortgage funnels, tax filing apps, and immigration forms.

slideName: Slide 18: Are You Scanning Mobile DOMs?
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• An accessible desktop site does not equal an accessible mobile site.
• DOMs change drastically at mobile breakpoints (accessible megamenus collapse into inaccessible mobile hamburger menus).
• The DOJ explicitly lists "mobile applications" alongside websites. Your scanner *must* test alternate viewports.

• Body Text:
• Web pages are getting better, but legal exposure remains massive for attached documents.
• Courts increasingly treat PDFs, spreadsheets, and slide decks hosted online as heavily scrutinized public accommodations.
• Remediating thousands of legacy PDFs is technically taxing and legally dangerous.

• Body Text:
• The vast majority of WCAG scanners exclusively crawl unauthenticated public marketing pages.
• The true legal exposure lies behind the login screen (patient charting, HR payroll, student registration).
• The DOJ does not stop at the login screen.

• Body Text:
• Traditional web crawlers get blocked by Single Sign-On (SSO), JWT, and multi-factor authentication.
• Modern tools (like wcagscanner.com) are built to natively authenticate, navigate user flows, and monitor deep, secure sections of web platforms just like a human user would.

header: How Do We Actually Fix This?
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• Compliance cannot rely on a single solution. It requires a synchronized triad:
• 1. The Manual Audit
• 2. The Automated Scan
• 3. Continuous Monitoring

header: AI is Not a Silver Bullet (Yet)
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• Fully trusting an AI to scan and auto-certify a site for compliance is highly risky.
• Why? Because AI struggles with context and dynamic interactions (The "Non-Deterministic Design" problem).
• AI chat interfaces and highly dynamic state-changes easily confuse automated tools.

header: The Danger of Auto-Generated Alt Text
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• Companies are rushing to use AI to auto-generate image alt text to pass automated scanners.
• The Trap: If an AI incorrectly tags a "Submit Payment" button as a "Decorative Image," the scanner passes it. But a blind user is completely blocked from checking out.
• Automated scanners will mistakenly flag hallucinated descriptions as "100% compliant."

slideName: Slide 25: Hybrid Automation: The Human in the Loop
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• Humans must remain in the loop for subjective evaluations: logical focus order, cognitive clarity, and contextual descriptions.
• Machines are for scale, humans are for nuance. Empathy cannot be automated.

• Body Text:
• Do not use AI to conduct the primary scan.
• Use AI for the patch.
• Once a human/automated scan identifies a failure, GenAI excels at writing the specific HTML/ARIA code patches to safely remediate templates at the framework level (React/Vue/Server-side).

• Body Text:
• One of the hardest parts of digital accessibility is taking a massive 500-page audit report and giving it to an engineering department.
• Accessibility shouldn't be a post-launch bottleneck; it needs to be integrated directly into the engineering workflow ("Shifting Left").

• Body Text:
• Stop sending massive PDFs of 1,000 accessibility errors to a "general issue" backlog.
• Tools like wcagscanner.com allow organizations to configure specific Scan Collections.
• Select isolated domains, specific components, or specific URLs, eliminating noise and the "not my job" syndrome.

• Body Text:
• "Point-in-time" audits fail because websites change daily (This creates "Repeat Defendants" in lawsuits).
• Integrate scanners via cron jobs and CI/CD pipelines to automatically deliver new scan results into specific Slack channels or Jira boards the moment code is deployed.
• Targeted delivery directly to the engineering team responsible.

slideName: Slide 30: Design Systems as a Defense
contentHtml:
• Body Text:
• Building accessibility primitives (compliant color tokens, pre-built ARIA patterns, keyboard-friendly focus states) directly into your company's core component library.
• When the design system is accessible, developers get WCAG compliance "for free" just by importing the component.

• Body Text:
• With the EAA becoming fully enforceable in June 2025, the standard for commerce is going global.
• U.S. businesses operating heavily in Europe must align their ADA Title III and DOJ Title II strategies with the rigid EAA mandates.

• Body Text:
• WCAG 3.0 is coming, moving from binary pass/fail mechanics to a holistic scoring system encompassing more complex disabilities.
• The Metaverse Challenge: With Apple Vision Pro and Meta Quest moving into enterprise, how do we apply 2D UI concepts like "keyboard focus" to 3D VR/AR spatial arrays?

• Body Text:
• Accessibility compliance is a civil right, a massive legal liability, and an untapped business opportunity.
• A cohesive strategy uses manual evaluation for empathy, automated scanning for scale, and continuous CI/CD integration for long-term protection.

• Body Text:
• Will Kirchheimer
• [Contact Info / Social Links]
